historical_cases.name: 2008–2010 Post-GFC K-Shape Consumer Split
historical_cases.peak_move: Luxury names (LVMH, Tiffany) recovered sharply by mid-2010 while mid-tier department stores continued to struggle; dollar stores (Family Dollar, Dollar Tree) posted multi-year outperformance against general retail benchmarks
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Broad labor market recovery and rising median wages between 2011–2012 began compressing the divergence as middle-income consumers regained purchasing power

historical_cases.name: 2001–2003 Post-Dot-Com / 9-11 Consumer Polarization
historical_cases.peak_move: High-end jewelry and experiential luxury held relatively firm among wealth-effect beneficiaries of still-elevated asset prices, while discount and off-price retail (Costco, TJX) gained sustained market share from squeezed middle-income households
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Housing-led wealth expansion after 2003 and the associated consumer credit boom re-inflated middle-income spending, narrowing the divergence

historical_cases.name: 2022–2023 Post-Pandemic Inflation Squeeze
historical_cases.peak_move: LVMH, Hermès, and Ferrari reported record revenues through 2022 and into early 2023 while Walmart and Dollar General flagged a distinct shift of formerly middle-income shoppers into their stores; off-price apparel (TJX, Ross) also outperformed department store peers
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Luxury demand began softening visibly by late 2023 as aspirational luxury buyers (upper-middle income) pulled back under cumulative inflation and rate pressure, while some goods-price disinflation partially relieved discount-tier pressure

historical_cases.name: 1990–1992 Recession-Era Consumer Bifurcation
historical_cases.peak_move: Neiman Marcus and high-end specialty retail maintained relatively stable revenues anchored by top-decile income households, while Wal-Mart's explosive expansion and warehouse club formats captured accelerating trade-down from squeezed middle-income consumers
historical_cases.exit_trigger: The 1993–1994 employment recovery and real wage stabilization restored middle-income discretionary capacity, compressing the polarization

differences.factor: Wealth-effect concentration via financial asset and real estate appreciation has become structurally more persistent due to index-driven passive investing and prolonged housing supply shortfalls, meaning the top quintile's balance sheet resilience may be more durable than in prior cycles

Elevated and sticky interest rates into 2025–2026 are compressing middle-income households through mortgage lock-in effects and high consumer credit costs simultaneously, a dual squeeze with fewer historical precedents in both magnitude and duration

Tariff escalation under the 2025 trade policy environment is introducing asymmetric cost pressures: luxury goods with pricing power and domestic-leaning supply chains may absorb tariff costs more easily than mass-market discretionary goods, potentially deepening the divergence through a cost-side channel rather than just an income channel

E-commerce price transparency and AI-driven deal-finding tools are accelerating the behavioral trade-down among middle-income consumers more efficiently than in prior cycles, structurally strengthening discount and off-price channel share gains beyond cyclical norms

similarities: The fundamental driver — income and wealth inequality widening during or after a macro stress event — is consistent with every prior historical episode, suggesting the polarization mechanism itself is not novel

Aspirational luxury (the upper-middle income cohort that bridges the two poles) has historically been the first segment to crack when macro pressure intensifies, consistent with observed softness in accessible luxury categories observed entering 2024–2025

Discount retailers historically gain sticky market share during polarization episodes, with a portion of trade-down behavior persisting even after the macro trigger eases, as observed in post-GFC Costco and Aldi penetration gains
this_time_different.observation: The current episode may be observed as unusually durable relative to historical analogs because wealth concentration, rate-driven middle-income squeeze, and tariff cost asymmetry are reinforcing the polarization from multiple structural directions simultaneously rather than through a single cyclical shock.

exit_signals: Sustained real wage growth at or above inflation for median and below-median income households for two or more consecutive quarters, observed in BLS and Atlanta Fed wage tracker data, which historically has preceded middle-income spending normalization

Meaningful deceleration in same-store sales or organic revenue growth at bellwether discount and off-price retailers (e.g., Walmart U.S., Dollar General, TJX), suggesting trade-down flows are plateauing rather than accelerating
Renewed volume growth (not just price) at mid-tier department stores and mass-market apparel chains, signaling that aspirational consumers are returning to mid-market channels

Luxury conglomerates reporting broad-based volume deceleration across geographies including the Americas and Europe (not solely China-driven softness), indicating even top-quintile discretionary budgets are being curtailed

A meaningful compression in the spread between consumer confidence readings for high-income versus low-income cohorts (e.g., Conference Board or University of Michigan sub-indices), historically a leading indicator that the K-shape is closing
duration_type_reasoning: Consumer spending polarization is a structural macro trend rooted in persistent wealth and income inequality dynamics rather than a single bounded event, and historically has required a multi-year labor market or credit cycle recovery to meaningfully compress.

real_world_timeline.description: The current episode of K-shaped consumer divergence became observable in early 2022 as post-pandemic inflation disproportionately squeezed middle- and lower-income households while asset-price-inflated top-quintile balance sheets remained resilient, driving simultaneous luxury strength and discount retail share gains.
